ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Vietnamese nem nuong vegetable roll. FOODON_03302051 (meat roll food product) is used as the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for nem nuong vegetable roll. A0CKP (Pork meat products) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for this specific Vietnamese grilled pork vegetable roll preparation.

allergens: gluten (possible from fish sauce or marinade)

serving.natural_language_portion: One serving consists of 2 grilled pork skewers (nem nướng) served with fresh vegetables, herbs, and rice paper or lettuce for wrapping, totalling approximately 180 g.

nutrient_source.title: Nem nuong (Vietnamese grilled pork sausage/skewer) — estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for grilled ground pork and Vietnamese fresh roll components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for nem nuong cuon rau exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated by component analysis: grilled ground pork (USDA FDC #167903, pork ground cooked), fresh vegetables and herbs (~15% of serving weight), rice vermicelli (~10%), and rice paper (~5%). Macros weighted by approximate ingredient proportions. This is a medium-confidence estimate. The dish is Vietnamese in origin and served at a Vietnamese restaurant in Singapore.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of rice vermicelli and mixed Vietnamese fresh roll components — analogous estimate based on rice vermicelli GI (58) and fresh vegetable/pork dilution effect; University of Sydney GI Database reference for rice noodles
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for nem nuong cuon rau. GI estimated at 48 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice vermicelli/rice noodles (GI ~58) diluted by the substantial protein and fat content from grilled pork and the low-GI fresh vegetable and herb components (~40% of serving). The protein and fat content significantly blunts the glycemic response. GL per serving calculated as (48 × 10.8 g carbohydrate per 100g × 1.8 serving factor) / 100 = approximately 9.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g) from fish sauce marinade and dipping sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one stick and request reduced fish sauce or dipping sauce on the side, as the sodium content from the marinade and condiments can contribute meaningfully to daily sodium targets of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~48) due to protein and fat content moderating carbohydrate absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Nem nuong cuon rau has a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 48 and a moderate glycemic load per serving (~9.3), making it a relatively suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in standard 2-stick portions without excess dipping sauce.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from grilled ground pork (~2.9 g per 100 g) and sodium from fish sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of the saturated fat and sodium content; choosing leaner pork preparations and limiting fish sauce-based dipping sauces can help keep this dish within heart-healthy dietary patterns.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nem nướng cuộn rau (grilled pork skewer vegetable roll) is a popular Vietnamese dish consisting of seasoned ground pork formed onto skewers, grilled until caramelised and slightly charred, then served alongside fresh lettuce or rice paper, herbs such as mint and coriander, cucumber, bean sprouts, and thin rice vermicelli. Diners wrap the grilled pork with the fresh accompaniments and dip the rolls into a sweet-savoury fish sauce-based dipping sauce (nước chấm). The dish offers a balance of smoky, savoury grilled meat with the freshness of raw vegetables and herbs. In Singapore, it is served at Vietnamese restaurants and is popular as a starter or light main course. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate protein (~13.5 g per 100 g) from the pork, moderate fat (~8.2 g per 100 g), and relatively low carbohydrate (~10.8 g per 100 g) from the vermicelli and rice paper components. The estimated glycemic index is low (~48), making it a reasonable option for blood glucose management. Sodium content is moderate to high due to fish sauce in the marinade and dipping sauce, which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The fresh vegetable components contribute dietary fibre, vitamin C, and micronutrients.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for nem nuong cuon rau exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated by component analysis using USDA FDC data for grilled ground pork and Vietnamese fresh roll components. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for rice vermicelli with protein/fat dilution adjustment. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are approved and the estimation methodology is documented and defensible.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component sources used for this estimation. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used. The food is Vietnamese in origin but classified under Singapore cuisine in the source GrabFood data because it is served at a Vietnamese restaurant in Singapore (Thien Long Vietnam Restaurant, Circular Road). The cuisine field has been corrected to Vietnamese to reflect the dish's true culinary origin. The serving size of 180 g is estimated for 2 sticks with accompanying vegetables and wrapping materials. Price data (SGD 15.90) is preserved from the GrabFood source record for reference only and is not used in nutritional analysis. The dish contains pork and is therefore not halal or kosher. Gluten may be present from fish sauce or soy-based marinades depending on restaurant preparation.
